2314	\section{Support and freeness predicates}
2315	\label{sec:quot_predicates}
2316	
2317	The Quot functor (\cref{def:quot_functor}) and the Grassmannian
2318	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) classify quotient sheaves subject to two
2319	geometric conditions: a coherent quotient must have \emph{schematic support
2320	proper over the base}, and the Grassmannian's quotients must be \emph{locally
2321	free of a fixed rank}. This section introduces both predicates.
2322	
2323	\begin{definition}
2324	
2325	\leanok
2326	[Annihilator ideal sheaf of a sheaf of modules]
2327	  \label{def:modules_annihilator}
2328	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.annihilator}
2329	  % NOTE: The definition carries no \uses: the Lean construction is
2330	  % `IdealSheafData.ofIdeals (fun U => Module.annihilator Γ(X,U) Γ(F,U))`, which is well-defined
2331	  % unconditionally and does NOT depend on the localization bridges at definition time. The two
2332	  % facts lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map and lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen are needed
2333	  % only for the downstream CHARACTERIZATION (the reverse inclusion / equality on affine opens, the
2334	  % "annihilator forward direction"), which is not yet a blueprint block.
2335	  % NOTE: The definition itself is formalized and axiom-clean; the inclusion direction
2336	  % \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U) \subseteq \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U))\)
2337	  % (\cref{lem:modules_annihilator_ideal_le}) is closed. The reverse inclusion, which identifies
2338	  % the annihilator sheaf with the module-annihilator on affine opens, remains bridge-gated on
2339	  % \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}.
2340	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
2341	  % Polynomials" (read from
2342	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L471).
2343	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2344	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
2345	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
2346	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
2347	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$."
2348	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
2349	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of
2350	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules that is \emph{quasi-coherent and of finite type}
2351	  (over a locally noetherian base, so that on each affine open \(U\) the section
2352	  module \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) is a finitely generated \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module).
2353	  The \emph{annihilator ideal sheaf} of \(\mathcal{F}\) is the ideal sheaf
2354	  \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\) defined on affine opens: for each affine open
2355	  \(U \subseteq X\),
2356	  \[
2357	    \mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)
2358	    \;=\; \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}\bigl(\mathcal{F}(U)\bigr)
2359	    \;\subseteq\; \mathcal{O}_X(U),
2360	  \]
2361	  the annihilator of the \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module \(\mathcal{F}(U)\). These
2362	  local data patch to a coherent ideal sheaf on \(X\) whose vanishing locus is
2363	  the set-theoretic support of \(\mathcal{F}\).
2364	
2365	  \emph{Well-definedness on basic opens.} The coherence condition for an ideal
2366	  sheaf requires that, for an affine open \(U\) and \(f \in \mathcal{O}_X(U)\),
2367	  the section \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)\) restricts correctly to the basic
2368	  open \(D(f) \subseteq U\), i.e. that
2369	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(D(f))}(\mathcal{F}(D(f)))\) is the extension of
2370	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U))\) along the localization map
2371	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(U) \to \mathcal{O}_X(D(f))\). This is exactly where the two
2372	  hypotheses enter. The bridge
2373	  \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen} supplies, for a quasi-coherent
2374	  finite-type \(\mathcal{F}\), that \(\mathcal{O}_X(D(f)) = (\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f\)
2375	  and that the restriction \(\mathcal{F}(U) \to \mathcal{F}(D(f))\) exhibits
2376	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(f))\) as the localized module \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}
2377	  \mathcal{F}(U)\); the algebra engine
2378	  \cref{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map} then yields, using that
2379	  \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) is finitely generated, the required identity
2380	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{(\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f}\bigl((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}
2381	  \mathcal{F}(U)\bigr) = \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U)) \cdot
2382	  (\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f\). Thus the local annihilators are compatible with basic-open
2383	  restriction, which is the patching datum that makes \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\)
2384	  a well-defined ideal sheaf. The finite-type hypothesis is genuinely required:
2385	  without finite generation of \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) the annihilator need not commute
2386	  with localization, and the local data fail to patch.
2387	
2388	  \emph{Note.} Mathlib does not carry an annihilator for sheaves of modules on a scheme.
2389	\end{definition}

2391	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2392	[Annihilator ideal bounded by the module annihilator on affine opens]
2393	  \label{lem:modules_annihilator_ideal_le}
2394	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.annihilator_ideal_le}
2395	  \uses{def:modules_annihilator}
2396	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(\mathcal{F}\) a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules and
2397	  \(U \subseteq X\) an affine open. Then the section over \(U\) of the annihilator
2398	  ideal sheaf \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\) (\cref{def:modules_annihilator}) is
2399	  contained in the module-annihilator of the sections of \(\mathcal{F}\) over
2400	  \(U\):
2401	  \[
2402	    \mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)
2403	    \;\subseteq\; \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}\bigl(\mathcal{F}(U)\bigr).
2404	  \]
2405	  This is the inclusion direction available directly from the definition of
2406	  \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\); the reverse inclusion is the basic-open coherence
2407	  statement gated on \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}. Proved directly
2408	  in Lean. It supports the well-definedness of the schematic support
2409	  (\cref{def:schematic_support}).

2411	
2412	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2413	[Annihilator of a finitely generated module commutes with
2414	  localization]
2415	  \label{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map}
2416	  \lean{Module.annihilator_isLocalizedModule_eq_map}
2417	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, let \(S \subseteq R\) be a multiplicative
2418	  submonoid, and let \(R_S = S^{-1}R\) be the localization of \(R\) at \(S\). Let
2419	  \(M\) be a \emph{finitely generated} \(R\)-module and let \(f : M \to M_S\) be
2420	  an \(R\)-linear map exhibiting \(M_S\) as the localized module \(S^{-1}M\)
2421	  (i.e.\ \(f\) is a localization of \(M\) at \(S\)). Then the annihilator of
2422	  \(M_S\) over \(R_S\) is the extension of the annihilator of \(M\) along the
2423	  structure map \(R \to R_S\):
2424	  \[
2425	    \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)
2426	    \;=\; (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S
2427	    \;=\; \mathrm{map}\bigl(R \to R_S\bigr)\bigl(\mathrm{Ann}_R M\bigr).
2428	  \]
2429	  Equivalently, for finitely generated \(M\) the annihilator commutes with
2430	  localization: \(\mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M) = S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M)\).
2431	
2432	  \emph{The finite-generation hypothesis is essential.} For a module that is not
2433	  finitely generated the inclusion \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M) \subseteq
2434	  \mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M)\) can be strict, so the identity fails. This is the
2435	  standard commutative-algebra fact that the annihilator of a finite module
2436	  localizes; it is recorded here as the load-bearing algebra engine for the
2437	  basic-open coherence of \cref{def:modules_annihilator}.
2438	\end{lemma}
2439	
2440	\begin{proof}
2441	  \(\supseteq\): if \(r \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\), then \(r\) kills every element of
2442	  \(M\), hence its image \(r/1 \in R_S\) kills every element of \(M_S\) (each such
2443	  element is \(m/s = (1/s)\cdot f(m)\) for some \(m \in M\), \(s \in S\), and
2444	  \((r/1)\cdot(m/s) = (1/s)\cdot f(r\,m) = 0\)). Thus
2445	  \((\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S \subseteq \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\).
2446	
2447	  \(\subseteq\): write \(M = \langle m_1, \dots, m_n\rangle\) using the
2448	  finite-generation hypothesis, and let \(y \in \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\). Write
2449	  \(y = a/s\) with \(a \in R\), \(s \in S\). For each generator \(m_i\) the element
2450	  \(y\cdot f(m_i) = (a/s)\cdot f(m_i) = 0\) in \(M_S\); by the definition of the
2451	  localized module this means there is \(u_i \in S\) with \(u_i\,a\,m_i = 0\) in
2452	  \(M\). Put \(u = \prod_{i=1}^n u_i \in S\). Then \(u\,a\) kills every generator
2453	  \(m_i\), hence kills all of \(M\), so \(u\,a \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\). Finally
2454	  \(y = a/s = (u\,a)/(u\,s)\) lies in \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}_R M =
2455	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\). Therefore \(\mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S) \subseteq
2456	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\), and combined with the reverse inclusion the two
2457	  ideals coincide. The use of a single common multiplier \(u = \prod_i u_i\) is
2458	  exactly the step that requires finitely many generators.
2459	\end{proof}

2485	  %             the PRIMARY irreducible gap, the QCoh(Spec R) ≃ Mod R essential-image gap)

2487	  %             DOWNSTREAM corollary of gap1 via isLocalizedModule_restrict_of_isIso_fromTildeΓ)
2488	  %   → this general-scheme keystone (lift across U ≅ Spec Γ(X,U), gap 2 transport).

2490	  % gap1 into all three IsLocalizedModule fields at once. gap1 itself reduces to the
2491	  % section-localization descent (\cref{lem:section_localization_descent}, keystone D), built from the
2492	  % slice-to-geometric bridge (\cref{lem:over_restrict_iso}, C) and the per-element local-tilde
2493	  % transport (\cref{lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_basicOpen_of_quasicoherent}, P1).
2494	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Schemes" (chapter tag 020J), \S 7
2495	  % "Quasi-coherent sheaves on affines", Lemma \texttt{lemma-spec-sheaves},
2496	  % item (4) (read from references/stacks-schemes.tex, L691-L702).
2497	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2498	  % "Let $R$ be a ring. Let $M$ be an $R$-module. Let $\widetilde M$
2499	  %  be the sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$-modules
2500	  %  associated to $M$.
2501	  %  [...]
2502	  %  \item For every $f\in R$ we have $\Gamma(D(f), \widetilde M) = M_f$
2503	  %  as an $R_f$-module."
2504	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Schemes", \S 7, Lemma on the sheaves
2505	  \(\widetilde M\) on \(\Spec(R)\), item (4).}
2506	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(\mathcal{M}\) be a \emph{quasi-coherent} sheaf
2507	  of modules on \(X\). Let \(U \subseteq X\) be an
2508	  affine open and \(f \in \Gamma(X, U)\). Then:
2509	  \begin{enumerate}
2510	    \item \(\Gamma\bigl(X, D(f)\bigr)\) is the localization \((\Gamma(X,U))_f\)
2511	      of \(\Gamma(X,U)\) away from \(f\)
2512	      (\cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}); and
2513	    \item the section-restriction map
2514	      \(\mathcal{M}(U) \to \mathcal{M}\bigl(D(f)\bigr)\) exhibits
2515	      \(\mathcal{M}(D(f))\) as the localized module
2516	      \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}\,\mathcal{M}(U)\); that is, it is a localization
2517	      of the \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-module \(\mathcal{M}(U)\) at the submonoid
2518	      \(\mathrm{powers}\,f = \{1, f, f^2, \dots\}\).
2519	  \end{enumerate}
2520	\end{lemma}
2521	
2522	\begin{proof}
2523	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
2524	  Part (1) is \cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}, the Mathlib statement
2525	  that on an affine open \(U\) the sections over the basic open \(D(f)\) form the
2526	  localization of \(\Gamma(X,U)\) away from \(f\).
2527	
2528	  For part (2), identify the affine open \(U\) with \(\Spec \Gamma(X, U)\). Over
2529	  this affine, a quasi-coherent sheaf is \(\widetilde{N}\) for the
2530	  \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-module \(N = \mathcal{M}(U)\), the sheaf associated to
2531	  \(N\) (no finiteness of \(N\) is required for this step). The Spec-local
2532	  computation of the sections of \(\widetilde{N}\) on a
2533	  basic open gives \(\Gamma\bigl(D(f), \widetilde{N}\bigr) = N_f\) as an
2534	  \((\Gamma(X,U))_f\)-module, and the restriction map
2535	  \(\Gamma(\Spec \Gamma(X,U), \widetilde{N}) = N \to N_f\) is precisely the
2536	  localization homomorphism \(N \to (\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1} N\). Transporting
2537	  this identification across the affine isomorphism
2538	  \(U \cong \Spec \Gamma(X, U)\) -- under which \(\mathcal{M}|_U\) corresponds to
2539	  \(\widetilde{N}\) by quasi-coherence and the basic open \(D(f) \subseteq U\)
2540	  corresponds to the standard open \(D(f) \subseteq \Spec\Gamma(X,U)\) -- shows
2541	  that the restriction \(\mathcal{M}(U) \to \mathcal{M}(D(f))\) is the
2542	  localization of \(N = \mathcal{M}(U)\) at \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\), i.e.\ it is a
2543	  localized module at \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\). The substantive content is this
2544	  transport of the Spec-local localization fact for the sheaf \(\widetilde{N}\)
2545	  to a general quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{M}\) via the affine identification.
2546	\end{proof}

2552	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Schemes" (tag 020J), \S 7, Lemma on the sheaves

2557	  \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}, part (2).}
2558	  Let \(N\) be an \(R\)-module and \(f \in R\). Then the presheaf restriction map of the
2559	  associated sheaf \(\widetilde{N}\) on \(\Spec R\) from the global sections
2560	  \(\Gamma(\widetilde{N}, \top)\) to the sections on the basic open \(\Gamma(\widetilde{N}, D(f))\)
2561	  exhibits \(\Gamma(\widetilde{N}, D(f))\) as the localized module
2562	  \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1} N\); that is, it is \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}(\mathrm{powers}\,f)\)
2563	  over \(R\).

2565	\begin{proof}

2568	  \(N \to \Gamma(\widetilde N, D(f))\). Since the global-sections map
2569	  \(N \to \Gamma(\widetilde N, \top)\) is an isomorphism and the canonical map to \(D(f)\) factors
2570	  through it followed by the restriction, precomposing the localization map with the inverse
2571	  isomorphism transfers the \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}\) property to the restriction map
2572	  (Mathlib's transfer lemma for localization along a linear equivalence).
2573	\end{proof}

4009	  % NOTE: the pinned Lean decl is `private` in the source (a project-internal helper for the
4010	  % G1-assemble step); the \lean{} pin is recorded for the DAG but resolves only project-internally.
4011	  \uses{lem:isBasis_basic_opens_mathlib, lem:stalkFunctor_map_injective_of_isBasis_mathlib,
4012	    lem:isIso_of_stalkFunctor_map_iso_mathlib}
4013	  \textit{Upgrades a per-basic-open isomorphism to an isomorphism of sheaves.}
4014	  Let \(\alpha : F \to G\) be a morphism of sheaves of \(R\)-modules on \(\Spec R\) whose component
4015	  on every basic open \(D(f)\) is an isomorphism. Then \(\alpha\) is an isomorphism.
4016	\end{lemma}
4017	\begin{proof}
4018	  \uses{lem:isBasis_basic_opens_mathlib, lem:stalkFunctor_map_injective_of_isBasis_mathlib,
4019	    lem:isIso_of_stalkFunctor_map_iso_mathlib}
4020	  The basic opens form a basis of \(\Spec R\) (\cref{lem:isBasis_basic_opens_mathlib}). Bijectivity
4021	  of every component \(\alpha_{D(f)}\) gives, in particular, injectivity on each basis open, hence
4022	  injectivity on every stalk (\cref{lem:stalkFunctor_map_injective_of_isBasis_mathlib});
4023	  surjectivity on stalks follows by lifting germs through the bijective basic-open components. A
4024	  morphism that is an isomorphism on every stalk is an isomorphism
4025	  (\cref{lem:isIso_of_stalkFunctor_map_iso_mathlib}).
4026	\end{proof}

4028	\begin{lemma}\leanok
4029	[$\mathrm{IsIso}\,M.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}$ from per-basic-open section localization
4030	(G1-assemble)]
4031	  \label{lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_isLocalizedModule_restrict}
4032	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_isLocalizedModule_restrict}
4033	  \uses{lem:isIso_sheaf_of_isIso_app_basicOpen, lem:bijective_comp_of_localizations,
4034	    lem:isLocalizedModule_tilde_restrict}
4035	  \textit{The G1-assemble reduction: section-localization on every basic open forces the
4036	  \(\widetilde{(-)}\)-counit to be an isomorphism.}
4037	  Let \(M\) be a sheaf of modules on \(\Spec R\), and suppose that for every \(f \in R\) the section
4038	  restriction \(\Gamma(M, \top) \to \Gamma(M, D(f))\) exhibits the target as
4039	  \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}(\mathrm{powers}\,f)\) over \(R\) -- exactly the conclusion of G1-core
4040	  (\cref{lem:qcoh_affine_section_localization}). Then the counit \(M.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\) is an
4041	  isomorphism.
4042	\end{lemma}
4043	\begin{proof}
4044	  \uses{lem:isIso_sheaf_of_isIso_app_basicOpen, lem:bijective_comp_of_localizations,
4045	    lem:isLocalizedModule_tilde_restrict}
4046	  Write \(N = \Gamma(M, \top)\). On each basic open \(D(f)\), the component of
4047	  \(\mathrm{modulesSpecToSheaf}(M.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ})\) is a map between two localizations of \(N\)
4048	  at \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\): the source \(\Gamma(\widetilde N, D(f))\) localizes \(N\) via the affine
4049	  tilde map (\cref{lem:isLocalizedModule_tilde_restrict}) and the target \(\Gamma(M, D(f))\)
4050	  localizes \(N\) by hypothesis, the two being intertwined by the compatibility
4051	  \(\mathrm{toOpen\_fromTildeΓ\_app}\). Hence the component is bijective
4052	  (\cref{lem:bijective_comp_of_localizations}), so an isomorphism. By
4053	  \cref{lem:isIso_sheaf_of_isIso_app_basicOpen} the sheaf morphism
4054	  \(\mathrm{modulesSpecToSheaf}(M.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ})\) is then an isomorphism, and since
4055	  \(\mathrm{modulesSpecToSheaf}\) is fully faithful it reflects isomorphisms, giving
4056	  \(\mathrm{IsIso}\,M.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\).
4057	\end{proof}

4085	  \uses{def:modules_annihilator, lem:modules_annihilator_ideal_le,
4086	    def:schematic_support_immersion}
4087	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
4088	  % Polynomials" (read from
4089	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L471).
4090	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
4091	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
4092	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
4093	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
4094	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$."
4095	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
4096	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of modules on a scheme \(X\). The
4097	  \emph{schematic support} of \(\mathcal{F}\) is the closed subscheme of \(X\)
4098	  cut out by its annihilator ideal sheaf (\cref{def:modules_annihilator}):
4099	  \[
4100	    \mathrm{schematicSupport}(\mathcal{F})
4101	    \;=\; V\bigl(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\bigr) \;\hookrightarrow\; X,
4102	  \]
4103	  equipped with its canonical closed immersion into \(X\). This realises
4104	  Nitsure's ``schematic support of \(\mathcal{F}\)'' as a closed subscheme of
4105	  \(X\).

4108	\begin{definition}\leanok
4109	[Schematic-support immersion]
4110	  \label{def:schematic_support_immersion}
4111	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.schematicSupportι}
4112	  \uses{def:schematic_support, def:modules_annihilator}
4113	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of modules on a scheme \(X\). The
4114	  \emph{schematic-support immersion} is the canonical closed immersion
4115	  \[
4116	    \mathrm{schematicSupport}(\mathcal{F}) \;\hookrightarrow\; X
4117	  \]
4118	  of the schematic support (\cref{def:schematic_support}) into the ambient
4119	  scheme, namely the subscheme immersion of the annihilator ideal sheaf
4120	  \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\) (\cref{def:modules_annihilator}). It is a
4121	  quasi-compact preimmersion onto the support, and it is the morphism whose
4122	  composite with a base morphism \(f : X \to S\) defines proper support
4123	  (\cref{def:has_proper_support}). Proved directly in Lean.
4124	\end{definition}

4126	\begin{lemma}[Properness as a morphism property]
4127	  \label{lem:isProper_mathlib}
4128	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsProper}
4129	  \mathlibok
4130	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
4131	  (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Proper}).}
4132	  A morphism \(f : X \to S\) of schemes is \emph{proper} iff it is separated,
4133	  universally closed, and locally of finite type. Properness is stable under base
4134	  change: if \(f : X \to S\) is proper and \(g : T \to S\) is any morphism then
4135	  the base change \(X \times_S T \to T\) is proper. Consequently the base-change
4136	  clause that Nitsure invokes (``properness \ldots\ preserved by base-change'')
4137	  applies directly to the proper-support condition below.
4138	\end{lemma}
4139	
4140	\begin{definition}
4141	
4142	\leanok
4143	[Proper support over the base]
4144	  \label{def:has_proper_support}
4145	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.HasProperSupport}
4146	  \uses{def:schematic_support, def:schematic_support_immersion, lem:isProper_mathlib}
4147	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "The Functors $\Hilb_{X/S}$ and
4148	  % $\Quot_{E/X/S}$" (read from
4149	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L407-L409
4150	  % + L418-L419).
4151	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
4152	  % "(i) a coherent sheaf $\F$ on $X_T = X\times_ST$
4153	  %  such that the schematic support of $\F$ is proper over $T$
4154	  %  and $\F$ is flat over $T$, together with
4155	  %  [...]
4156	  %  Then as properness and flatness are preserved
4157	  %  by base-change, and as tensor-product is right exact,"
4158	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
4159	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of modules on a scheme \(X\) and let
4160	  \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism. We say \(\mathcal{F}\) \emph{has proper support
4161	  over \(S\) (along \(f\))} when the composite morphism
4162	  \[
4163	    \mathrm{schematicSupport}(\mathcal{F}) \hookrightarrow X \xrightarrow{f} S
4164	  \]
4165	  is proper (\cref{lem:isProper_mathlib}). Since properness is stable under base
4166	  change (\cref{lem:isProper_mathlib}), this condition is preserved by pull-back
4167	  along any \(S'\to S\), which is exactly what the well-definedness of the Quot
4168	  functor's pullback action requires.
4169	\end{definition}

4171	\begin{definition}
4172	
4173	\leanok
4174	[Locally free of rank \(d\)]
4175	  \label{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}
4176	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.SheafOfModules.IsLocallyFreeOfRank}
4177	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, "Elementary Examples, Exercises (2)" and
4178	  % "Grassmannian of a vector bundle" (read from
4179	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L562-L564
4180	  % + L584-L585).
4181	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
4182	  % "$\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
4183	  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$.
4184	  %  [...]
4185	  %  the set of all equivalence classes $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients
4186	  %  $q: E_T \to \F$ where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$,"
4187	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2) (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
4188	  Let \(\mathcal{M}\) be a sheaf of modules on a scheme \(X\) and let
4189	  \(d \in \mathbb{N}\). We say \(\mathcal{M}\) is \emph{locally free of rank
4190	  \(d\)} when there exists an open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(X\) together with
4191	  isomorphisms \(\mathcal{M}|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}^{\oplus d}\) for

4197	\section{The Quot functor}
4198	\label{sec:quot_functor}
4199	
4200	\begin{definition}\leanok
4201	[Quot functor]
4202	  \label{def:quot_functor}
4203	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QuotFunctor}
4204	  \uses{def:hilbert_polynomial, def:has_proper_support}
4205	
4206	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "The Functors \(\Hilb_{X/S}\) and
4207	  % \(\Quot_{E/X/S}\)" (read from
4208	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L394-L433
4209	  % + L481-L498).
4210	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
4211	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme and let \(X\to S\) be a finite type
4212	  %  scheme over it. Let \(E\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X\).
4213	  %  Let \(Sch_S\) denote the category of all locally noetherian schemes
4214	  %  over \(S\). For any \(T\to S\) in \(Sch_S\), a \textbf{family of quotients of \(E\)
4215	  %  parametrised by \(T\)} will mean
4216	  %  a pair \((\F,q)\) consisting of
4217	  %
4218	  %  (i) a coherent sheaf \(\F\) on \(X_T = X\times_ST\)
4219	  %  such that the schematic support of \(\F\) is proper over \(T\)
4220	  %  and \(\F\) is flat over \(T\), together with
4221	  %
4222	  %  (ii) a surjective \({\mathcal O}_{X_T}\)-linear homomorphism of sheaves
4223	  %  \(q:E_T  \to \F\) where \(E_T\) is the pull-back of \(E\) under
4224	  %  the projection \(X_T \to X\).
4225	  %
4226	  %  Two such families \((\F,q)\) and \((\F,q)\) parametrised by \(T\) will
4227	  %  be regarded as equivalent if \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\)),
4228	  %  and \(\langle \F, q \rangle\) will denote
4229	  %  an equivalence class. Then as properness and flatness are preserved
4230	  %  by base-change, and as tensor-product is right exact,
4231	  %  the pull-back of \(\langle \F, q \rangle\) under an \(S\)-morphism \(T'\to T\) is
4232	  %  well-defined, which gives
4233	  %  a set-valued contravariant functor
4234	  %  \(\Quot_{E/X/S} : Sch_S \to Sets\) under which
4235	  %  \(\)T\mapsto  \{\mbox{ All } \langle \F, q \rangle \mbox{ parametrised by }T\}\(\)
4236	  %  [...]
4237	  %  This shows that the functor \(\Quot_{E/X/S}\) naturally decomposes
4238	  %  as a co-product
4239	  %  \(\)\Quot_{E/X/S} = \coprod_{{\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]}\, \Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\(\)
4240	  %  where for any polynomial \({\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]\), the functor
4241	  %  \(\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\) associates
4242	  %  to any \(T\) the set of all equivalence classes of
4243	  %  families \(\langle \F,q \rangle\) such that at each \(t\in T\) the
4244	  %  Hilbert polynomial of the restriction \(\F_t\), calculated
4245	  %  using the pull-back of \(L\), is \({\Phi}\)."
4246	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
4247	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
4248	  \(L\) a line bundle on \(X\), \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\), and
4249	  \(\Phi \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\). The \emph{Quot functor with Hilbert
4250	  polynomial \(\Phi\)}
4251	  \[
4252	    \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S} : (\mathrm{Sch}/S)^{op} \longrightarrow \mathbf{Set}
4253	  \]
4254	  sends an \(S\)-scheme \(T \to S\) to the set of equivalence classes
4255	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) of pairs \((\mathcal{F}, q)\) where
4256	  \begin{enumerate}
4257	    \item \(\mathcal{F}\) is a coherent sheaf on \(X_T = X \times_S T\) whose
4258	      schematic support is proper over \(T\) (\cref{def:has_proper_support}) and
4259	      which is flat over \(T\);
4260	    \item \(q : E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) is a surjective
4261	      \(\mathcal{O}_{X_T}\)-linear homomorphism, where \(E_T\) is the pull-back
4262	      of \(E\) along \(X_T \to X\);
4263	    \item for every \(t \in T\) the Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}\)
4264	      with respect to \(L|_{X_t}\) (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}) equals
4265	      \(\Phi\).
4266	  \end{enumerate}
4267	  Two pairs \((\mathcal{F}, q)\) and \((\mathcal{F}', q')\) are equivalent iff
4268	  \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). On morphisms \(f : T' \to T\) over \(S\), the functor
4269	  acts by pulling back the quotient along \(X_{T'} \to X_T\) (well-defined
4270	  since tensor product is right-exact and preserves both flatness and
4271	  properness). The Hilbert scheme is the special case
4272	  \(\Hilb^{\Phi,L}_{X/S} := \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}\).
4273	\end{definition}

4275	\section{The Grassmannian scheme}
4276	\label{sec:grassmannian_scheme}
4277	
4278	The Quot construction proceeds by embedding \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) as a
4279	locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian over \(S\). Since Mathlib carries
4280	no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} (only a finite-rank-quotient variety in
4281	linear algebra), we package the construction here.
4282	
4283	\begin{definition}\leanok
4284	[Grassmannian scheme]
4285	  \label{def:grassmannian_scheme}
4286	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Grassmannian}
4287	  \uses{def:quot_functor, def:is_locally_free_of_rank}
4288	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, "Elementary Examples, Exercises~(2)" and
4289	  % "Construction of Grassmannian" (read from
4290	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L546-L599
4291	  % + L773-L850).
4292	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
4293	  % "For any integers \(r\ge d\ge 1\), an explicit construction
4294	  %  the Grassmannian scheme
4295	  %  \(\grass(r,d)\) over \(\Z\), together with the tautological
4296	  %  quotient \(u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U\) where \
4297	  %  \(\U\) is a rank \(d\) locally free sheaf on \(\grass(r,d)\),
4298	  %  has been given at the end of this section. [...]
4299	  %  Show that \(\grass(r,d)\) together with the quotient
4300	  %  \(u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U\) represents the
4301	  %  contravariant functor
4302	  %  \(\)\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}\(\)
4303	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any \(T\) the
4304	  %  set of all equivalence classes
4305	  %  \(\langle \F, q\rangle\) of quotients \(q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F\)
4306	  %  where \(\F\) is a locally free sheaf on \(T\) of rank \(d\).
4307	  %  [...]
4308	  %  Using the above show that, more generally, if \(S\) is a scheme and
4309	  %  \(E\) is a locally free \({\mathcal O}_S\)-module of rank \(r\),
4310	  %  the functor \(\Grass(E,d)=\Quot_{E/S/S}^{d,{\mathcal O}_S}\)
4311	  %  on all \(S\)-schemes which
4312	  %  by definition associates to any \(T\)
4313	  %  the set of all equivalence classes \(\langle \F, q\rangle\) of quotients
4314	  %  \(q: E_T \to \F\) where \(\F\) is a locally free sheaf on \(T\) of rank \(d\),
4315	  %  is representable. The representing scheme
4316	  %  is denoted by \(\grass(E,d)\) and is called the rank \(d\)
4317	  %  relative Grassmannian of \(E\) over \(S\)."
4318	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2) and "Construction of
4319	  Grassmannian" (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
4320	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme and let \(V\) be a locally free
4321	  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module of rank \(r\). For an integer \(1 \le d \le r\), the
4322	  \emph{Grassmannian of rank-\(d\) quotients of \(V\)} is the functor
4323	  \[
4324	    \mathrm{Grass}(V, d) : (\mathrm{Sch}/S)^{op} \longrightarrow \mathbf{Set},
4325	    \qquad
4326	    T \longmapsto \{ \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle :
4327	    q : V_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F},\; \mathcal{F}
4328	    \text{ locally free of rank } d \}
4329	  \]
4330	  with equivalence \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\); the rank-\(d\) local freeness of
4331	  \(\mathcal{F}\) is as in \cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}. Concretely,
4332	  \(\mathrm{Grass}(V, d) = \Quot^{d,\mathcal{O}_S}_{V/S/S}\): the Quot functor
4333	  (\cref{def:quot_functor}) in the special case \(X = S\), \(E = V\), constant
4334	  Hilbert polynomial \(\Phi = d\).
4335	\end{definition}

4342	  Let \(\mathcal{C}\) be a category and \(F : \mathcal{C}^{op} \to \mathbf{Set}\)

4346	  \(\mathrm{Hom}_{\mathcal{C}}(-, Y) \cong F\) (the Yoneda bijection).

4406	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(V\) a locally free \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module
4407	  of rank \(r\), and \(1 \le d \le r\). The functor \(\mathrm{Grass}(V,d)\) of
4408	  \cref{def:grassmannian_scheme} is representable by a smooth projective
4409	  \(S\)-scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d) \to S\) of relative dimension \(d(r-d)\),
4410	  equipped with a tautological quotient
4411	  \(\pi^* V \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) of rank \(d\). The determinant
4412	  line bundle \(\det(\mathcal{U})\) is relatively very ample, and the
4413	  associated linear system gives a closed embedding
4414	  \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d) \hookrightarrow
4415	  \mathbb{P}_S\!\left(\bigwedge^d V\right)\) -- the \emph{Pl\"ucker embedding}.
4416	
4417	\end{theorem}
4418	
4419	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the "Construction of Grassmannian" sub-section of
4420	% [Nitsure], \S 1 (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
4421	% L773-L930), which gives the construction in five named steps
4422	% ("Construction by gluing together affine patches", "Separatedness",
4423	% "Properness", "Universal quotient", "Projective embedding"). The total
4424	% verbatim text is ~150 lines; it is reproduced piecewise in the
4425	% SOURCE QUOTE comment of the theorem statement above.
4426	\begin{proof}
4427	  
4428	  Over \(S = \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) and \(V = \mathcal{O}_S^{\oplus r}\) the
4429	  Grassmannian \(\mathrm{Gr}(r, d)\) is constructed by gluing
4430	  \(\binom{r}{d}\) affine charts \(U^I = \Spec\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) indexed by
4431	  size-\(d\) subsets \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\). The chart \(U^I\)
4432	  carries the matrix \(X^I\) of size \(d \times r\) whose \(I\)-th \(d \times d\)
4433	  minor is the identity, with the remaining \(d(r-d)\) entries free
4434	  variables (so \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\)). The
4435	  transition maps \(\theta_{I,J} : U^I \cap U^J \to U^J \cap U^I\) defined
4436	  by \(X^J \mapsto (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) on the locus where \(\det X^I_J\) is
4437	  invertible satisfy the cocycle condition
4438	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{I,J} \circ \theta_{J,K}\). The glued
4439	  \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec\mathbb{Z}\) is smooth of relative dimension
4440	  \(d(r-d)\). Separatedness is checked from the diagonal
4441	  \(\Delta_{I,J} \subset U^I \times U^J\) cut out by \(X^J_I X^I - X^J = 0\).
4442	  Properness follows from the valuative criterion for DVRs: given
4443	  \(\varphi : \Spec K \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over a DVR \(R\) with fraction
4444	  field \(K\), factoring through some chart \(U^I\) via a ring map
4445	  \(f : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to K\), choose \(J\) minimising the valuation
4446	  \(\nu(f(P^I_J))\) of the minor; on the chart \(U^J\) all entries have
4447	  non-negative valuation, so \(f\) extends uniquely to \(\Spec R\).
4448	
4449	  The universal quotient \(u : \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\oplus r}
4450	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) is given on \(U^I\) by the matrix \(X^I\)
4451	  and glued by \(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1} \in GL_d(U^I \cap U^J)\). The
4452	  determinant line bundle \(\det\mathcal{U}\) has transition functions
4453	  \(\det g_{I,J} = 1/P^I_J\); the global sections
4454	  \(\sigma_I \in \Gamma(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d), \det\mathcal{U})\) defined by
4455	  \(\sigma_I|_{U^J} = P^J_I\) form a base-point-free linear system separating
4456	  points over \(\Spec\mathbb{Z}\), giving an embedding into
4457	  \(\mathbb{P}^{\binom{r}{d}-1}_{\mathbb{Z}}\), which is closed by
4458	  properness. Representability of \(\mathrm{Grass}(r,d)\) is then verified
4459	  on the universal pair \((\mathrm{Gr}(r,d), u)\): a quotient
4460	  \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\oplus r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of
4461	  rank \(d\) on \(T\) is everywhere locally trivialised; on the locus where
4462	  the columns indexed by \(I\) are a basis the matrix of \(q\) has the form
4463	  \(X^I\), defining the classifying morphism \(T \to U^I \subset
4464	  \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\). Base change from \(\mathbb{Z}\) to a general noetherian
4465	  base \(S\) with a vector bundle \(V\) is direct: trivialise \(V\) on an open
4466	  cover and glue. For non-trivial \(V\) the same construction produces
4467	  \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d)\) with Pl\"ucker embedding into
4468	  \(\mathbb{P}_S(\bigwedge^d V)\).
4469	\end{proof}

426	
427	This section builds, bottom-up, toward the keystone
428	`lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen`: for a quasi-coherent sheaf of modules `M`
429	on a scheme `X`, an affine open `U`, and `f ∈ Γ(X,U)`, the restriction
430	`M(U) → M(D(f))` exhibits `M(D(f))` as `IsLocalizedModule (powers f)` over `Γ(X,U)`.
431	
432	The substance is the affine (Spec-local) computation: over `Spec R`, a quasi-coherent
433	sheaf is `Ñ = tilde N` for `N = Γ(M)`, and the basic-open restriction of `Ñ` is the
434	module localization map. Mathlib's `AlgebraicGeometry.tilde` namespace already carries

2413	[Annihilator of a finitely generated module commutes with
2414	  localization]
2415	  \label{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map}
2416	  \lean{Module.annihilator_isLocalizedModule_eq_map}
2417	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, let \(S \subseteq R\) be a multiplicative
2418	  submonoid, and let \(R_S = S^{-1}R\) be the localization of \(R\) at \(S\). Let
2419	  \(M\) be a \emph{finitely generated} \(R\)-module and let \(f : M \to M_S\) be
2420	  an \(R\)-linear map exhibiting \(M_S\) as the localized module \(S^{-1}M\)
2421	  (i.e.\ \(f\) is a localization of \(M\) at \(S\)). Then the annihilator of
2422	  \(M_S\) over \(R_S\) is the extension of the annihilator of \(M\) along the
2423	  structure map \(R \to R_S\):
2424	  \[
2425	    \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)
2426	    \;=\; (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S
2427	    \;=\; \mathrm{map}\bigl(R \to R_S\bigr)\bigl(\mathrm{Ann}_R M\bigr).
2428	  \]
2429	  Equivalently, for finitely generated \(M\) the annihilator commutes with
2430	  localization: \(\mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M) = S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M)\).
2431	
2432	  \emph{The finite-generation hypothesis is essential.} For a module that is not
2433	  finitely generated the inclusion \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M) \subseteq
2434	  \mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M)\) can be strict, so the identity fails. This is the
2435	  standard commutative-algebra fact that the annihilator of a finite module
2436	  localizes; it is recorded here as the load-bearing algebra engine for the
2437	  basic-open coherence of \cref{def:modules_annihilator}.
2438	\end{lemma}
2439	
2440	\begin{proof}
2441	  \(\supseteq\): if \(r \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\), then \(r\) kills every element of
2442	  \(M\), hence its image \(r/1 \in R_S\) kills every element of \(M_S\) (each such
2443	  element is \(m/s = (1/s)\cdot f(m)\) for some \(m \in M\), \(s \in S\), and
2444	  \((r/1)\cdot(m/s) = (1/s)\cdot f(r\,m) = 0\)). Thus
2445	  \((\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S \subseteq \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\).
2446	
2447	  \(\subseteq\): write \(M = \langle m_1, \dots, m_n\rangle\) using the
2448	  finite-generation hypothesis, and let \(y \in \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\). Write
2449	  \(y = a/s\) with \(a \in R\), \(s \in S\). For each generator \(m_i\) the element
2450	  \(y\cdot f(m_i) = (a/s)\cdot f(m_i) = 0\) in \(M_S\); by the definition of the
2451	  localized module this means there is \(u_i \in S\) with \(u_i\,a\,m_i = 0\) in
2452	  \(M\). Put \(u = \prod_{i=1}^n u_i \in S\). Then \(u\,a\) kills every generator
2453	  \(m_i\), hence kills all of \(M\), so \(u\,a \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\). Finally
2454	  \(y = a/s = (u\,a)/(u\,s)\) lies in \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}_R M =
2455	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\). Therefore \(\mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S) \subseteq
2456	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\), and combined with the reverse inclusion the two
2457	  ideals coincide. The use of a single common multiplier \(u = \prod_i u_i\) is
2458	  exactly the step that requires finitely many generators.
2459	\end{proof}
